source.title: Event-related EEG/MEG synchronization and desynchronization: basic principles

confidence_rationale: Foundational paper establishing the ERD/ERS framework and the 'idling rhythm' interpretation of beta: beta synchronizes when a cortical area is not engaged in active processing and desynchronizes during voluntary movement. This challenges MECH-090 in an important way -- if beta is primarily an idling/disengagement rhythm, then elevated beta during a committed sequence would indicate the motor cortex is *not* engaged, which contradicts the committed-maintenance gating account. Confidence 0.55 reflects genuine tension: the evidence against is real but the idling interpretation applies most clearly to sensorimotor beta, while MECH-090 targets prefrontal/BG beta during cognitive commitment -- a distinction subsequent work (Engel & Fries 2010; Helfrich & Knight 2019) explicitly makes.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Beta and alpha oscillations in sensorimotor cortex synchronize (event-related synchronization, ERS) when a cortical region is in an 'idling' state -- not actively engaged in sensory processing or movement. Event-related desynchronization (ERD) precedes and accompanies voluntary movement. Post-movement beta rebound (PMBR) reflects return to the resting/idling state after action completion. Under this framework, elevated beta = cortical idling, suppressed beta = active cortical engagement.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-090 claims elevated beta during committed sequences gates E3 output propagation. The idling interpretation inverts this: if elevated beta means the motor region is disengaged, then high beta during an ongoing committed sequence would indicate the motor circuit is off-line -- not actively maintaining commitment. The post-movement beta rebound (PMBR) under the idling account reflects recovery from engagement rather than restoration of a maintained state. This weakens the committed-sequence-maintenance framing specifically for sensorimotor beta; the challenge is less severe for prefrontal/BG beta (the locus of MECH-090) where the idling account is less well-established.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The idling account has been substantially refined since 1999. Subsequent work distinguishes sensorimotor beta (idling-consistent) from prefrontal and BG beta (status-quo/maintenance-consistent), largely rehabilitating MECH-090 for its specific circuit locus. The weakening is real but scoped: it applies to motor cortex beta most directly, while MECH-090's gating mechanism is located in the BG-prefrontal commitment circuit. This entry documents the challenge rather than refutes the claim.
mapping.source_context: Review of human EEG/MEG studies; primary sensorimotor cortex; voluntary movement, motor imagery, and sensory stimulation paradigms. Foundational ERD/ERS framework paper.

confidence_components.notes: Very high source quality -- foundational methods and framework paper; mapping fidelity is moderate because the idling account targets sensorimotor beta while MECH-090 targets prefrontal/BG beta; transfer risk is elevated because the claim domain (cognitive commitment gating) is different from the source domain (sensorimotor idling). Confidence 0.55 reflects a genuine but bounded challenge.
